nativeRange.habitat: Found on shallow to moderately deep coral reefs at depths ranging from 3 to 100 meters.

diet.notes: The French Angelfish accepts a wide variety of foods and should be offered a varied diet including Spirulina, marine algae, high-quality angelfish preparations containing sponge matter, mysis shrimp, and frozen shrimp. Feeding at least three times daily is recommended. In the wild, adults consume sponges, algae, bryozoans, zoantharians (colonial anemone-like invertebrates), gorgonians, and tunicates.

imageCandidates.notes: Main adult species photo from FishBase summary page; filename Popar_ub.jpg confirms Pomacanthus paru. License not explicitly stated on the summary page.
Photo by LASZLO ILYES from Cleveland, Ohio, USA. Taken underwater at Grote Knip, Curaçao on December 31, 2012. Confirms species identity as Pomacanthus paru. High-quality underwater photograph.
Product hero image from LiveAquaria; large format, clean composition typical of retailer product photography.

summary: The French Angelfish (Pomacanthus paru) is a large marine angelfish with dark blue-black scales edged in yellow and a pale blue face. Semi-aggressive and not reef-safe, it needs a tank of at least 946 liters with ample live rock.
careNotes: The French Angelfish (Pomacanthus paru) is one of the larger marine angelfish, reaching up to 41 cm in the wild. It tends to dominate an aquarium and requires a very large tank — at least 946 liters (roughly 250 gallons) — with generous amounts of live rock for cover and natural grazing. This species is NOT reef safe: it will nip at sessile invertebrates, including soft corals, stony corals, and clam mantles, and should not be housed with them. A varied diet offered at least three times daily keeps this fish in good condition. Adults are territorial and tend to form monogamous pairs; in the wild, juveniles act as cleaner fish, picking parasites and debris from larger fish at established cleaning stations.
breedingNotes: In the wild, adult French Angelfish form monogamous, territorial spawning pairs and release their eggs into open water (pelagic spawning), where the eggs drift with the current before hatching. Juveniles are well known for acting as cleaner fish at cleaning stations, servicing larger species. Captive breeding has rarely been documented and is considered an advanced undertaking.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (41.1 cm TL vs LiveAquaria's ~38 cm — minor disagreement, used FishBase max), temperature range (22.9–28°C vs LiveAquaria 22.2–25.6°C — minor disagreement, used conservative intersection), native range, taxonomy, lifespan (27 years), and diet composition. FishBase also describes territorial pair-forming behavior consistent with semi-aggressive temperament.

fish.breedingNotes: In the wild, adults spawn as monogamous pairs, releasing eggs into open water where they develop pelagically. Juveniles are well-documented cleaners at cleaning stations. Captive breeding is rarely achieved and is considered advanced.
